sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Peripheral intravenous (IV) cannulation provides vascular access for fluid and medication administration. Prehospital IV access should not delay scene time for time-critical conditions (e.g. STEMI, trauma); it should be established en route when possible.

sections.title: Site Selection
sections.content:
**Preferred sites (proximal to distal):**
- Antecubital fossa (cephalic, basilic, median cubital veins) - large bore access, rapid infusion
- Forearm (cephalic, basilic)
- Dorsum of hand
- External jugular (for difficult access or cardiac arrest)

**Avoid:**
- Injured limb, limb with dialysis fistula, post-mastectomy limb
- Over joints (increases dislodgement risk)
- Lower extremities (risk of thrombophlebitis)

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Gather equipment: appropriate cannula (14–22G), tourniquet, alcohol swab, dressing, flush

2. Apply tourniquet 10–15 cm above site; ask patient to clench fist

3. Visualise and/or palpate vein; clean with alcohol swab and allow to dry

4. Bevel up, advance at 10–30° until flashback of blood

5. Lower angle slightly; advance cannula 1–2 mm further to ensure tip is in lumen

6. Advance plastic cannula fully while withdrawing needle

7. Release tourniquet; apply finger pressure proximal to tip; remove stylet

8. Attach extension set or cap; flush with 5–10 mL 0.9% NaCl

9. Secure with transparent dressing; label with date/time/gauge

10. Dispose of needle immediately in sharps container

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Use largest bore cannula the vein can accommodate in high-acuity patients (14–18G for resuscitation)
- Two large-bore cannulae in antecubital fossa provide equivalent flow to a central line
- Confirm patency with flush before administering medication
- Maximum 2 attempts before reassessing site or calling for assistance
